about.title: About Me
about.bio: I am Chiicake, a 2026 new-grad backend engineer with roughly 2 years of backend experience. I mainly work with Rust, Go, and Java, and I also spend time around Redis, PostgreSQL, and Docker.
about.bioExtra: The problems I care about most are service reliability, distributed systems, protocols and security mechanisms, and cache or storage paths. I keep writing blog posts, building small experiments, and reading infrastructure or systems code. I am still early in my career, but the technical curiosity is very real.

skills.title: Capability Matrix
skills.relatedBlogsEmpty: A slot is reserved here for future articles tied to this capability.

matrices.title: Reliable Backend Service Design
matrices.summary: Design backend systems around contracts, service boundaries, and failure handling so they can evolve without turning fragile.
focus: Service boundary definition and responsibility control
scenarios: Long-lived backend capabilities with multiple consumers

matrices.title: Distributed Reliability Engineering
matrices.summary: Turn retries, idempotency, rate limiting, and recovery into first-class system capabilities instead of patching them in later.
focus: Rate limiting, circuit breaking, and capacity protection

matrices.title: Storage and Performance Experiments
matrices.summary: I like drilling down into hot paths, cache behavior, and protocol details when a performance problem becomes structurally important.
focus: Latency hotspot diagnosis and performance profiling
Protocols, signatures, and security boundary analysis
scenarios: Security protocol and signature verification work

matrices.title: Platform Delivery and Engineering Systems
matrices.summary: Turn deployment, monitoring, and operational rules into reusable team capabilities so delivery depends less on manual coordination.
focus: Monitoring, logging, and service health systems

featuredProject.title: hybird-kv
featuredProject.tagline: An SSD-friendly high-performance KV storage experiment built around a hybrid architecture: a kernel hot-key cache plus a user-space authoritative engine.
featuredProject.summary: I am using it as a systems-heavy workbench to test how hot and cold paths should be separated, how cache policy can be decoupled from the storage core, and where the boundary between kernel space and user space should sit if you want hit efficiency without sacrificing evolvability or operational clarity.
highlights: Push the hottest access path forward so frequent reads and writes do not keep paying unnecessary user-space cost.
Keep the user-space engine authoritative so control logic, observability, and recovery remain understandable.
Treat eviction policy as a pluggable plane so different workload tradeoffs can be explored without rewriting the storage core.

modules.name: Kernel hot-key cache
modules.description: Handles the most latency-sensitive hot read and write path.

modules.name: User-space engine
modules.description: Owns authoritative data, write orchestration, and recovery behavior.

modules.name: Policy plane
modules.description: Hosts replaceable cache policy and eviction strategy experiments.
